quiz_questions.question_text: The sympathetic division of the ANS is often called the:

options.text: Voluntary motor system
Fight or flight system
Feed and breed system
Rest and digest system

quiz_questions.explanation: The sympathetic division prepares the body for emergency situations (fight or flight) by increasing heart rate, dilating pupils, redirecting blood to skeletal muscles, and releasing stored energy.

quiz_questions.question_text: The parasympathetic division is often called the:

options.text: Fight or flight system
Rest and digest system
Stress response system
Voluntary control system

quiz_questions.explanation: The parasympathetic division promotes calm, restorative functions including digestion, nutrient absorption, energy storage, and slowing the heart rate during periods of rest and relaxation.

quiz_questions.question_text: Sympathetic activation causes heart rate to:

options.text: Remain unchanged
Decrease significantly
Stop completely
Increase to pump more blood to muscles

quiz_questions.explanation: Sympathetic stimulation increases heart rate and contractility through norepinephrine acting on beta-1 adrenergic receptors on cardiac pacemaker and muscle cells, ensuring adequate blood delivery to active skeletal muscles during stress.

quiz_questions.question_text: Parasympathetic activation causes heart rate to:

options.text: Become irregular
Decrease via the vagus nerve
Increase significantly
Remain unchanged

quiz_questions.explanation: The parasympathetic vagus nerve (CN X) releases acetylcholine onto muscarinic receptors on the SA and AV nodes of the heart, slowing the heart rate to conserve energy during rest.

quiz_questions.question_text: The primary neurotransmitter released by sympathetic postganglionic neurons is:

options.text: Acetylcholine (cholinergic transmitter)
Serotonin (5-hydroxytryptamine)
Norepinephrine (noradrenaline)
Dopamine (catecholamine precursor)

quiz_questions.explanation: Most sympathetic postganglionic neurons release norepinephrine (NE), which acts on adrenergic receptors (alpha and beta) on target organs to produce fight-or-flight responses such as increased heart rate, bronchodilation, and vasoconstriction.

quiz_questions.question_text: All preganglionic neurons in both the sympathetic and parasympathetic divisions release:

options.text: Dopamine
Epinephrine
Acetylcholine (ACh)
Norepinephrine

quiz_questions.explanation: Acetylcholine is the universal neurotransmitter at all autonomic preganglionic synapses (both sympathetic and parasympathetic), acting on nicotinic receptors on postganglionic neuron cell bodies in the ganglia.

quiz_questions.question_text: Sympathetic stimulation causes the pupils to:

options.text: Remain unchanged
Dilate (mydriasis) to let in more light
Constrict (miosis)
Close completely

quiz_questions.explanation: Sympathetic activation causes pupil dilation (mydriasis) by stimulating the dilator pupillae muscle, allowing more light to enter the eye and improving visual awareness during stressful or threatening situations.

quiz_questions.question_text: The adrenal medulla, when stimulated by sympathetic neurons, releases:

options.text: Only insulin secreted into the portal circulation
Only cortisol released from the adrenal cortex
Only acetylcholine from parasympathetic nerve endings
Epinephrine (adrenaline) and norepinephrine into the bloodstream

quiz_questions.explanation: The adrenal medulla is essentially a modified sympathetic ganglion that releases epinephrine (~80%) and norepinephrine (~20%) directly into the bloodstream, prolonging and amplifying the fight-or-flight response throughout the entire body.

quiz_questions.question_text: Sympathetic preganglionic neurons originate from which region of the spinal cord?

options.text: Cervical segments (C1-C8)
Sacral segments (S2-S4)
Thoracolumbar segments (T1-L2)
Brainstem cranial nerve nuclei

quiz_questions.explanation: Sympathetic preganglionic neurons originate from the lateral horn of the thoracolumbar spinal cord (T1 through L2), which is why the sympathetic division is also called the thoracolumbar outflow.

quiz_questions.question_text: Parasympathetic preganglionic neurons originate from:

options.text: Brainstem cranial nerve nuclei and sacral spinal cord (S2-S4)
Thoracolumbar spinal cord (T1-L2)
Lumbar spinal cord only (L1-L5)
Cervical spinal cord only (C1-C8)

quiz_questions.explanation: Parasympathetic preganglionic neurons originate from cranial nerve nuclei in the brainstem (CN III, VII, IX, X) and the sacral spinal cord (S2-S4), which is why this division is called the craniosacral outflow.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the autonomic nervous system (ANS)?

options.text: The involuntary division of the nervous system that regulates cardiac muscle, smooth muscle, and glandular activity to maintain homeostasis
The portion of the nervous system that processes sensory information in the brain
The network of cranial nerves that control facial expressions
The division of the nervous system that controls voluntary skeletal muscle movement

quiz_questions.explanation: The autonomic nervous system is the involuntary motor division of the PNS that controls visceral functions through its sympathetic and parasympathetic branches, regulating heart rate, digestion, respiration, and other functions without conscious effort.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'dual innervation' mean?

options.text: Two neurotransmitters are always released at the same synapse
Each nerve contains both sensory and motor fibers
Most organs receive both sympathetic and parasympathetic input, which typically have opposing effects
Each organ receives nerve supply from two different spinal cord levels

quiz_questions.explanation: Dual innervation means most visceral organs receive nerve fibers from both the sympathetic and parasympathetic divisions, which generally have opposing (antagonistic) effects, allowing fine-tuned, dynamic control of organ function.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'adrenergic' mean in the context of the ANS?

options.text: Relating to the production of adrenaline only in the brain
Relating to neurons or receptors that use norepinephrine (or epinephrine) as their neurotransmitter
Relating to neurons that release acetylcholine as their neurotransmitter
Relating to the adrenal cortex and its production of cortisol

quiz_questions.explanation: Adrenergic refers to neurons that release norepinephrine (noradrenaline) or epinephrine (adrenaline) and the receptors they activate; most sympathetic postganglionic neurons are adrenergic, acting on alpha and beta adrenergic receptors.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'cholinergic' mean in the context of the ANS?

options.text: Relating to neurons that release norepinephrine
Relating to cholesterol metabolism in nerve tissue
Relating to neurons or receptors that use acetylcholine as their neurotransmitter
Relating to the breakdown of choline in the liver

quiz_questions.explanation: Cholinergic refers to neurons that release acetylcholine (ACh), including all preganglionic neurons (both divisions), all parasympathetic postganglionic neurons, and somatic motor neurons; ACh acts on nicotinic and muscarinic receptors.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a beta-blocker?

options.text: A drug that increases norepinephrine release from sympathetic neurons
A drug that stimulates sympathetic beta receptors to increase heart rate
A drug that blocks all parasympathetic activity
A drug that blocks sympathetic beta-adrenergic receptors, thereby lowering heart rate and blood pressure

quiz_questions.explanation: Beta-blockers are drugs (such as metoprolol and atenolol) that block beta-adrenergic receptors on the heart and blood vessels, reducing the effects of sympathetic stimulation and thereby lowering heart rate, contractility, and blood pressure.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'muscarinic receptor' refer to?

options.text: A type of acetylcholine receptor found on effector organs of the parasympathetic system (heart, smooth muscle, glands)
A receptor that responds to the neurotransmitter GABA
A type of adrenergic receptor found on skeletal muscle
A receptor in skeletal muscle that detects stretch

quiz_questions.explanation: Muscarinic receptors are a class of acetylcholine receptors (named for their sensitivity to muscarine) found on target organs innervated by parasympathetic postganglionic neurons, including the heart (M2), smooth muscle, and glands.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'thoracolumbar outflow' mean?

options.text: The motor fibers controlling thoracic and lumbar muscles
The sensory fibers entering the thoracic and lumbar spinal cord
The parasympathetic preganglionic fibers that exit from the brainstem and sacral cord
The sympathetic preganglionic fibers that exit from the thoracic and upper lumbar segments (T1-L2) of the spinal cord

quiz_questions.explanation: Thoracolumbar outflow describes the origin of sympathetic preganglionic neurons from the lateral horn of spinal segments T1 through L2; these fibers exit via ventral roots to synapse in paravertebral or prevertebral ganglia before reaching target organs.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'craniosacral outflow' mean?

options.text: The sympathetic fibers that exit from the thoracic and lumbar spinal cord
The sensory fibers from the face and pelvis
The parasympathetic preganglionic fibers that exit from the brainstem (cranial nerves III, VII, IX, X) and sacral spinal cord (S2-S4)
The somatic motor fibers that control head and pelvic muscles

quiz_questions.explanation: Craniosacral outflow describes the origin of parasympathetic preganglionic neurons from cranial nerve nuclei (CN III, VII, IX, X) in the brainstem and the lateral horn of sacral segments S2-S4, which is why the parasympathetic division is also called the craniosacral division.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a nicotinic receptor?

options.text: A type of acetylcholine receptor found at autonomic ganglia and the neuromuscular junction that is stimulated by both ACh and nicotine
A norepinephrine receptor found on smooth muscle
A dopamine receptor in the brain's reward center
A receptor that only responds to nicotine from tobacco products

quiz_questions.explanation: Nicotinic receptors are ligand-gated ion channels activated by acetylcholine (and nicotine) found at all autonomic ganglia (both sympathetic and parasympathetic) and at the skeletal muscle neuromuscular junction.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'homeostasis' mean in the context of ANS function?

options.text: The process of growing new neurons to replace damaged ones
The ability of the body to fight off infections
The maintenance of a stable internal environment through dynamic balance of sympathetic and parasympathetic activity
A state of complete inactivity in the nervous system

quiz_questions.explanation: Homeostasis is the maintenance of stable internal conditions (such as heart rate, blood pressure, body temperature, and blood glucose) through the dynamic, push-pull balance between sympathetic and parasympathetic activity that constantly adjusts to changing demands.

quiz_questions.question_text: A jogger is suddenly chased by an aggressive dog. Her heart pounds, her breathing quickens, her pupils dilate, and she feels a surge of energy. Which division of the ANS is responsible for these coordinated responses, and what hormone amplifies the effect?

options.text: Somatic nervous system; testosterone provides the energy surge
Parasympathetic division; cortisol amplifies the response
Parasympathetic division; insulin provides the energy surge
Sympathetic division; epinephrine (adrenaline) released from the adrenal medulla amplifies and prolongs the response

quiz_questions.explanation: The sympathetic division orchestrates the fight-or-flight response, and the adrenal medulla (acting as a modified sympathetic ganglion) releases epinephrine and norepinephrine into the bloodstream, amplifying and prolonging the effects throughout the body.

quiz_questions.question_text: After eating a large Thanksgiving dinner, a person feels relaxed and drowsy while digesting the meal. Their heart rate slows, salivation increases, and intestinal motility increases. Which ANS division predominates in this scenario, and through which major nerve?

options.text: Somatic nervous system via spinal nerves to the GI tract
Sympathetic division via the splanchnic nerves
Sympathetic division via the sympathetic trunk
Parasympathetic division via the vagus nerve (CN X), promoting rest-and-digest functions

quiz_questions.explanation: The parasympathetic division dominates during rest and digestion; the vagus nerve (CN X) is the primary parasympathetic nerve that slows the heart, increases gastric motility and secretion, and promotes nutrient absorption in the GI tract.

quiz_questions.question_text: A patient with hypertension is prescribed a beta-blocker (metoprolol). The patient asks why this medication lowers blood pressure. How does blocking beta-adrenergic receptors reduce blood pressure?

options.text: It increases the production of acetylcholine in the brain
It blocks sympathetic beta-1 receptors on the heart, reducing heart rate and contractility, and blocks beta-2 receptors on blood vessels, reducing vasoconstriction
It directly relaxes skeletal muscles to reduce physical tension
It increases parasympathetic activity to the kidneys, causing more fluid excretion

quiz_questions.explanation: Beta-blockers reduce blood pressure by blocking beta-1 adrenergic receptors on the heart (decreasing heart rate and force of contraction) and by reducing renin release from the kidneys, thereby lowering both cardiac output and peripheral vascular resistance.

quiz_questions.question_text: An anesthesiologist administers atropine to a patient before surgery because the patient's heart rate dropped to 42 bpm during intubation. The atropine rapidly increases the heart rate to 78 bpm. What receptor does atropine block, and why does blocking it increase heart rate?

options.text: Atropine blocks alpha-adrenergic receptors, causing vasodilation that reflexively increases heart rate
Atropine blocks nicotinic receptors at the neuromuscular junction, causing the heart muscle to contract faster
Atropine blocks beta receptors, removing sympathetic braking on the heart
Atropine blocks muscarinic ACh receptors on the heart, removing parasympathetic (vagal) inhibition and allowing the heart's intrinsic rate to increase

quiz_questions.explanation: Atropine is a muscarinic antagonist that blocks ACh from binding to muscarinic (M2) receptors on the SA and AV nodes; this removes the parasympathetic vagal tone that normally slows the heart, allowing the heart's intrinsic pacemaker rate (~100 bpm) and sympathetic input to dominate.

quiz_questions.question_text: A patient with severe asthma is having difficulty breathing because their bronchioles are constricted. The physician administers albuterol, a beta-2 agonist, through an inhaler. How does this medication relieve the asthma symptoms from an ANS perspective?

options.text: It stimulates alpha receptors on the diaphragm to increase breathing force
It mimics sympathetic stimulation by activating beta-2 adrenergic receptors on bronchial smooth muscle, causing bronchodilation
It blocks parasympathetic constriction of the bronchioles by inhibiting ACh
It blocks all norepinephrine release in the lungs

quiz_questions.explanation: Albuterol is a beta-2 adrenergic agonist that mimics the sympathetic nervous system's effect on the airways; activation of beta-2 receptors on bronchial smooth muscle causes relaxation and bronchodilation, opening the airways and improving airflow.

quiz_questions.question_text: A trauma surgeon notes that a patient in hemorrhagic shock has a rapid heart rate, pale and cool skin, and dilated pupils. These signs are all due to a massive sympathetic response. What is the physiological purpose of each of these responses?

options.text: The rapid heart rate worsens the bleeding while the other signs are coincidental
These responses are parasympathetic attempts to slow bleeding
Tachycardia maintains cardiac output despite low blood volume; vasoconstriction to the skin redirects blood to vital organs; pupil dilation improves visual awareness
They are random stress responses with no physiological purpose

quiz_questions.explanation: In hemorrhagic shock, the sympathetic system activates compensatory mechanisms: tachycardia maintains cardiac output with reduced blood volume, peripheral vasoconstriction (causing pale, cool skin) shunts blood to vital organs, and pupil dilation enhances visual awareness of threats.

quiz_questions.question_text: A medical student is studying a patient with a pheochromocytoma, a tumor of the adrenal medulla that continuously secretes large amounts of epinephrine and norepinephrine. What symptoms would you expect this patient to have?

options.text: Excessive digestion, diarrhea, and slow heart rate from parasympathetic overdrive
Severe hypertension, tachycardia, excessive sweating, anxiety, and headaches from continuous sympathetic overstimulation
Chronic low blood pressure, slow heart rate, and excessive sleepiness
Only weight gain and fatigue from excess cortisol

quiz_questions.explanation: A pheochromocytoma causes continuous secretion of catecholamines (epinephrine and norepinephrine), producing sustained sympathetic overstimulation with severe episodic or persistent hypertension, tachycardia, diaphoresis (sweating), headaches, and anxiety.

quiz_questions.question_text: A nursing student observes that a patient receiving an IV infusion of a drug suddenly develops an extremely slow heart rate (20 bpm). The nurse calls the physician, who orders atropine immediately. Why is atropine the appropriate emergency treatment, and what would happen if a beta-blocker were given instead?

options.text: Atropine blocks parasympathetic vagal slowing to allow the heart rate to increase; a beta-blocker would worsen bradycardia by also reducing sympathetic stimulation
Atropine stimulates the parasympathetic system; a beta-blocker would have the same beneficial effect
Atropine is a beta agonist that directly stimulates the heart; a beta-blocker would be equally effective
Both drugs would increase heart rate through the same mechanism

quiz_questions.explanation: Atropine blocks muscarinic receptors to remove parasympathetic braking, allowing heart rate to increase. A beta-blocker would be contraindicated because it would block the sympathetic drive that is helping maintain whatever heart rate remains, potentially causing dangerous further slowing or cardiac arrest.

quiz_questions.question_text: A patient undergoing a stressful job interview notices his mouth is extremely dry and his hands are sweaty. He finds this contradictory since one area is dry and another is wet. How does the ANS explain both symptoms simultaneously?

options.text: The sympathetic system controls saliva while the parasympathetic controls sweat
Dry mouth is from dehydration and sweating is from fever, not the ANS
Sympathetic activation inhibits salivary gland secretion (dry mouth) while simultaneously stimulating sweat glands (sweaty palms), as both are stress responses
The parasympathetic system is causing both symptoms through the same mechanism

quiz_questions.explanation: During sympathetic (stress) activation, salivary gland secretion is reduced (producing thick, scanty saliva and dry mouth), while eccrine sweat glands are stimulated by sympathetic cholinergic fibers (a notable exception to the usual norepinephrine rule), causing sweating especially on the palms.

quiz_questions.question_text: A physician explains to a patient that the autonomic nervous system uses a 'two-neuron chain' to reach target organs, unlike the somatic system which uses a single motor neuron. A student asks why this two-neuron design is beneficial. What is the advantage?

options.text: The ganglionic synapse between the two neurons allows for signal modulation, integration, and divergence, enabling widespread coordinated organ responses
It prevents any voluntary control over autonomic functions
It allows faster signal transmission than a single neuron pathway
It simply provides a backup neuron in case the first one is damaged

quiz_questions.explanation: The two-neuron chain (preganglionic to postganglionic) provides an additional synapse in the ganglion where signals can be modulated, integrated, and distributed to multiple postganglionic neurons, allowing a single preganglionic neuron to coordinate widespread, simultaneous responses across multiple target organs.
